notable.notable_blurb: Yale 1702 first M.A.; Durham minister 50 years

narrative_blocks.detail: The son of a Harvard graduate and great-grandson of Harvard's second president, Nathaniel had prepared for college under his uncle Rev. Israel Chauncy of Stratford — one of Yale's founders. After receiving his degree he taught at Hopkins Grammar School in Hadley and Springfield, then settled in Durham, Connecticut, as its first minister in 1706. He served Durham until his death 50 years later, in 1756. He was a Yale Fellow (1746-1752) and a published preacher — his election sermon of 1719 and his defense of regular singing (1728) circulated widely. In the theological disputes of his era he led the 'Old Light' faction. His sister Sarah married Rev. Samuel Whittlesey of Wallingford, connecting this branch to the Hooker family through the Newton line. (Yale Biographical Sketches)
narrative_blocks.body: At Yale's inaugural commencement on September 13, 1702, Nathaniel Chauncey presented himself for a Bachelor's degree. The Corporation examined him and found him worthy of the higher degree — awarding him the M.A. instead. He was the only candidate at that first commencement to receive a degree by examination rather than transfer.

narrative_blocks.header: He came for a bachelor’s degree
narrative_blocks.body: Presenting himself for a B.A. in 1702, Chauncey was advanced on the spot to Yale’s first Master of Arts — the college’s first degree awarded by examination. He preached at Durham for fifty years and sat as a Yale Fellow.

narrative_blocks.header: He came for a bachelor’s degree
narrative_blocks.body: Presenting himself for a B.A. in 1702, Chauncey was advanced on the spot to Yale’s first Master of Arts — the college’s first degree awarded by examination. He preached at Durham for fifty years and sat as a Yale Fellow.

narrative_blocks.detail: Chauncy was educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, and served as a Church of England minister before emigrating under Puritan pressure. At Plymouth he became minister of Scituate, then succeeded Henry Dunster as Harvard's president in 1654. He held the post for 18 years. His son Nathaniel (X01902) graduated Harvard in 1661 and became minister of Hatfield, Massachusetts. His grandson Nathaniel Chauncey (X01904) received Yale's first M.A. degree in 1702 and served as Durham's minister for 50 years. His great-granddaughter Sarah Chauncey married Rev. Samuel Whittlesey (X01900) of Wallingford, whose son Chauncey Whittlesey (I00074) married Martha Newton — a 4th-generation descendant of Thomas Hooker. (Wikipedia)
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Chauncy served as the second President of Harvard College from 1654 until his death in 1672. A Cambridge-educated Puritan who emigrated to Plymouth Colony in 1638, he was one of the most rigorous classical scholars in early New England — and the patriarch of a remarkable dynasty of Yale and Harvard clergy.
The Trinity College, Cambridge scholar emigrated to Plymouth Colony in 1638 and took Harvard’s presidency in 1654 — holding it eighteen years until his death in office in 1672, at seventy-nine.
